summary:
The Taj Mahal (1632–1653 AD) is the supreme achievement of Mughal architecture and one of the most celebrated buildings in human history — a white marble mausoleum commissioned by [[event:2506|Shah Jahan]] on the banks of the Yamuna River at Agra, built to honor his beloved wife Mumtaz Mahal who died in childbirth in 1631. The building represents the culmination of an architectural tradition that had developed through [[event:2381|Akbar's]] Fatehpur Sikri and Jahangir's gardens, combining Persian, Ottoman, Indian, and Central Asian architectural elements into a perfect synthesis. The design, attributed to the architect Ustad Ahmad Lahauri, achieved a symmetry and visual harmony that is remarkable from any vantage point: the central dome (73 meters high) is surrounded by four identical minarets, set on a raised plinth, within a formal Charbagh (four-part paradise garden) with a central reflecting pool.

The construction was extraordinary in scale and craftsmanship: approximately 20,000 workers were employed over 22 years; the white Makrana marble was brought from Rajasthan (320 km), while semi-precious stones for the inlay work (pietra dura) — lapis lazuli, carnelian, malachite, jade, onyx — were sourced from across Asia and the Middle East. The calligraphy adorning the entrance archways was executed by the calligrapher Amanat Khan; the gardens' water system used an ingenious underground pipe and tank system to maintain consistent pressure. Shah Jahan, who was later imprisoned by his son [[event:2507|Aurangzeb]] in the Red Fort of Agra, reportedly spent his final years gazing at the Taj Mahal from his window across the river. The building was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1983 and is India's most visited monument.
